findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update `src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/README.plan-review.md` (or add a new CLAUDE.md / ADR entry) to document the calibration corpus contract: PORT-F*/PORT-N* naming, four-label `note` schema, operator-attestation AC, and the kappa ≥ 0.70 / recall 1.0 / FAR 0.0 release-gate defaults.
evidence: ABSENCE: the plan lists files in scope as only the eval YAML, the prompt, and one test file — no README, ADR, or CLAUDE.md update is mentioned
The existing `src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/README.plan-review.md` covers the override mechanism but is not updated to cover the calibration workflow
Novel decisions introduced: four-label `note` schema, PORT-F*/PORT-N* naming convention, operator-attestation AC pattern, specific kappa/stability thresholds
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new calibration pattern — balanced 8-case corpus, mandatory four-label `note` convention, operator-attested AC, and kappa ≥ 0.70 release gate — that future criterion authors would need to replicate, but no documentation update (README, ADR, or CLAUDE.md) is planned to capture this as a reusable contract.
findings.location: Approach section; Acceptance Criteria (all AC lines referencing the eval spec structure)
scenarios: A future criterion author needs to add calibration and has no documented pattern to follow, leading to inconsistent corpus structures or missing operator-attestation gates
A reviewer cannot tell whether the kappa threshold is a project-wide standard or a one-off choice without a doc reference
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Calibration corpus contract' section to `src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/README.plan-review.md` that specifies: (1) balanced must-fire / must-not-fire split, (2) required `note` label schema, (3) gold_set requirements, (4) the standard release-gate thresholds, and (5) the operator-attestation AC template. Reference this section from the new eval YAML's header comment.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge in the plan that the four-label note schema is fully novel (no existing infrastructure validates it) and either: (a) define the exact parsing rule the `finding_contract` test will use (e.g. regex pattern), or (b) note that the bespoke test is the sole guard and that misspellings in note labels will silently pass `validate_eval_spec`.
evidence: Search for 'Plan citation: Alternate shape: Causal mechanism: Observable breakage:' returned zero matches across the entire repo
eval.py:223 confirms 'note' is in metadata_keys (a passthrough field) — its content is never validated by the eval infrastructure
validate_eval_spec (eval.py:275) and validate_dataset_and_gold (eval.py:202) have no logic to inspect note field content
No existing eval spec uses these four label names — the schema is entirely novel to this plan
findings.finding: The plan introduces a four-label structured schema embedded inside the `note` field (`Plan citation:`, `Alternate shape:`, `Causal mechanism:`, `Observable breakage:`) but no existing infrastructure in `validate_eval_spec`, `validate_dataset_and_gold`, or any registered scorer validates this schema. The plan's `finding_contract` test is designed to parse and assert this structure directly from YAML — but if the four-label format is misparsed (e.g. note is a multi-line scalar that YAML folds) or a label is misspelled, neither the eval loader nor `validate_eval_spec` will catch it; only the bespoke test catches it. This is not merely a documentation convention: the plan treats these labels as a testable contract ('does not rely on validate_eval_spec to interpret note content'), yet the labels themselves have no canonical definition anywhere in the codebase to verify against.
findings.location: Approach section: 'The note is one non-empty string containing the exact labels Plan citation:, Alternate shape:, Causal mechanism:, and Observable breakage: with non-whitespace text after every label'
scenarios: An implementer misspells 'Causal mechanism:' as 'Causal Mechanism:' (capital M); validate_eval_spec passes, the finding_contract test is the only guard — a test that must itself be authored correctly to catch this.
YAML multi-line scalar folding strips a newline between labels, making label detection regex-sensitive; no infrastructure catches this outside the bespoke test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the four required `note` label strings (`Plan citation:`, `Alternate shape:`, `Causal mechanism:`, `Observable breakage:`) as named constants (e.g., in `test_project_portability_eval.py` or a shared fixture) co-located with the `finding_contract` test logic, so the parsing rule and the schema it validates are in the same place.
evidence: Plan text: 'The note is one non-empty string containing the exact labels `Plan citation:`, `Alternate shape:`, `Causal mechanism:`, and `Observable breakage:` with non-whitespace text after every label'

Plan text: 'The `finding_contract` test loads the YAML directly and asserts every `PORT-F*` note is a string whose four exact labels are each followed by non-whitespace text; it does not rely on `validate_eval_spec` to interpret note content.'
Codebase: `metadata_keys` in eval.py line 223 treats `note` as opaque free text — no sub-schema or parsing protocol is defined for it anywhere in the codebase.

All existing `note:` usages in eval specs (plan-review-verifier, plan-review-container, etc.) are simple strings, not compound four-label structures — this format is novel and unspecified as a named schema.
findings.finding: The plan defines a novel four-label compound schema for the `note` field (exact labels: `Plan citation:`, `Alternate shape:`, `Causal mechanism:`, `Observable breakage:`) but this parsing protocol is never co-located with the dataset-case schema — it exists only as Approach prose and is validated only by the `finding_contract` test's ad-hoc string inspection. No YAML schema block, type annotation, or named constant defines the four required labels, so an implementer authoring the PORT-F* `note` strings has no machine-checkable contract to work from until the test itself fails.
findings.location: Approach section — `note` compound sub-schema description; AC: `finding_contract` test
scenarios: An implementer authors the PORT-F* note strings without knowing the exact label spellings and only discovers the mismatch when the `finding_contract` test runs — no early authoring-time validation.
A future maintainer adding a PORT-F5 case has no schema definition to reference, only the test's regex logic.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a module-level tuple or frozenset `REQUIRED_NOTE_LABELS = ('Plan citation:', 'Alternate shape:', 'Causal mechanism:', 'Observable breakage:')` in `tests/unit/test_project_portability_eval.py` and reference it in both the `finding_contract` assertion and the plan's Approach description.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a prerequisite guard to the final AC, e.g., 'This AC requires the criterion-integration child to be merged first; skip or xfail `test_project_portability_criterion.py` until that dependency lands', or split it into a separate post-merge verification step.
evidence: 'Depends on the criterion-integration child because calibration executes the activated project criterion and its authored rubric.'
The proving command includes `tests/unit/test_project_portability_criterion.py` which is not listed in the Scope of this ticket and does not exist until the dependency lands.
VERIFY-COMMAND DEFECT TAXONOMY #9: 'references a sibling ticket's files with no dependency edge [guard]'
findings.finding: The final AC's proving command references `tests/unit/test_project_portability_criterion.py`, a file that belongs to the named dependency child ticket (`immune-floury-toad` / criterion-integration child) and does not exist until that child completes. If the executor runs this AC before the dependency lands, the command silently fails with a collection error, not a meaningful test failure. The plan names the dependency but does not guard the AC from premature execution.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — last item: 'The final project rubric retains every structural marker specified by `immune-floury-toad`. Prove with `.venv/bin/pytest -q tests/unit/test_project_portability_criterion.py tests/unit/test_project_portability_eval.py`'
scenarios: Executor runs the final AC command before the child ticket merges; pytest reports 'ERROR collecting tests/unit/test_project_portability_criterion.py — no such file', and the AC appears broken rather than blocked.
A CI check runs the full test suite on this ticket's branch, fails on the missing file, and obscures whether the rubric-marker assertion itself is correct.
findings.suggested_fix: Annotate the AC: '[requires criterion-integration child merged] Prove with `.venv/bin/pytest -q tests/unit/test_project_portability_criterion.py tests/unit/test_project_portability_eval.py`'. Alternatively, add a pytest `skipif` guard in `test_project_portability_eval.py` that skips the cross-file assertion when `test_project_portability_criterion.py` does not exist.

scenarios: An implementer completing this plan has no instructions to author `test_project_portability_criterion.py`, yet the final gate requires it to pass — the AC will fail for reasons outside this plan's scope

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `model: anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-6` in the eval spec is intentional (the field is currently inert with only a deterministic scorer), or align it with the established `anthropic:claude-opus-4-8` convention used by all other packaged eval specs to avoid a latent grader-discipline failure if an llm-judge is ever added.
evidence: All 15 packaged eval specs use `model: anthropic:claude-opus-4-8` (Partially-verified: model string format is valid, but Sonnet-as-eval-grader is an unestablished surface).
src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/plan-review-finder.eval.yaml line 11: `model: anthropic:claude-opus-4-8`
Since the spec declares only one deterministic scorer (`emits_valid_findings`), the model field is currently unused at runtime — impact is low unless an llm-judge is added later.
Evidence class: Partially-verified (model string format valid; grader-role usage for Sonnet unconfirmed against established convention).
findings.finding: The eval spec specifies `model: anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-6`, but every one of the 15 existing packaged eval specs uses `anthropic:claude-opus-4-8` as the eval/grader model. In this spec, however, the single declared scorer is `emits_valid_findings` (deterministic), which does not invoke the model at all — so the model field is currently inert. If a future iteration adds an llm-judge scorer (as prior art specs do), the grader choice diverges from the established convention without justification, and the grader-discipline check (`_family` cross-family check) would need to pass for Sonnet-as-grader against a Sonnet generator.
scenarios: A future calibration iteration adds an llm-judge scorer, at which point the grader-discipline cross-family check must pass for Sonnet-grading-Sonnet — which the existing `_family` check would reject.
A reader following the packaged eval spec pattern expects opus-class graders and may be confused by the divergence.
findings.suggested_fix: Change `model: anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-6` to `model: anthropic:claude-opus-4-8` to match the grader convention in all existing eval specs, or add an explicit comment explaining why Sonnet is chosen here.

findings.finding: `tests/unit/test_project_portability_criterion.py` is referenced in AC 15 as a required proof artifact ('Prove with `.venv/bin/pytest -q tests/unit/test_project_portability_criterion.py tests/unit/test_project_portability_eval.py`') but this file does not exist in the repository (confirmed by directory listing of `tests/unit/`), is absent from the plan's Scope section (which names only `tests/unit/test_project_portability_eval.py`), and is never described as something this plan creates. The plan references a component whose structure and content are entirely undefined.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 15 (final AC); Scope section
scenarios: AC 15 cannot be attested: the `pytest` command will exit non-zero because the file does not exist, blocking completion even if all other work is correct.
An implementer does not know what tests to write for `test_project_portability_criterion.py` because its content is never specified in this plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the exact expected metric values for a perfect injected solve separately from the live-run thresholds (e.g., 'injected test asserts recall==1.0, FAR==0.0, agreement==1.0, per-case stability==1.0, kappa==1.0; live run gate is recall>=1.0, FAR==0.0, kappa>=0.70').
evidence: 'An injected perfect solve returns the exact metric mapping specified in the Approach'
Approach: 'per-case stability >= 0.6666666667, and Cohen kappa >= 0.70'

A perfect inject over 8 balanced cases yields recall=1.0, FAR=0.0, agreement=1.0, per-case stability=1.0, kappa=1.0 — but the plan conflates the live threshold (>= 0.70) with the deterministic expected output
findings.finding: The `calibration_metrics` AC says 'returns the exact metric mapping specified in the Approach', but the Approach states only inequality thresholds for two of the five metrics (per-case `stability >= 0.6666666667`, kappa `>= 0.70`). An executor writing the deterministic injected test cannot infer whether to assert `kappa == 1.0` (the natural perfect-solve value) or `kappa >= 0.70` (the live-run gate). The 'exact metric mapping' phrase is contradicted by the inequality notation, creating a real ambiguity about what the test should assert.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'An injected perfect solve returns the exact metric mapping specified in the Approach'; Approach paragraph listing recall/FAR/agreement/stability/kappa
scenarios: An executor writes `assert metrics['kappa'] >= 0.70` instead of `== 1.0`, making the test pass trivially even for a broken implementation that scores kappa=0.75.
An executor writes `assert metrics['stability'] >= 0.6666...` leaving the exact injected value unspecified, weakening the determinism guarantee.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach, add a sentence such as: 'The injected perfect-solve test asserts exact values: recall=1.0, FAR=0.0, agreement=1.0, per-case stability=1.0, kappa=1.0. The live-run release gate checks recall >= 1.0, FAR == 0.0, agreement == 1.0, per-case stability >= 0.6666666667, kappa >= 0.70.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the `calibration_metrics` AC: state explicitly that a perfect injected solve over 4 fire + 4 no-fire cases yields `kappa == 1.0` and `stability_min == 1.0` (not merely `>= threshold`), matching the prior-art pattern in `test_calibrate_perfect_criterion_kappa_one`.
evidence: Plan Approach: 'The release threshold is recall `1.0`, false-accept rate `0.0`, `agreement: 1.0`, per-case `stability >= 0.6666666667`, and Cohen kappa `>= 0.70`.'
AC text: 'An injected perfect solve returns the exact metric mapping specified in the Approach.'

Codebase `test_criteria_eval.py` line 83: `assert r['recall'] == 1.0 and r['false_accept'] == 0.0` and `assert r['agreement'] == 1.0 and r['kappa'] == 1.0` — the prior art asserts exact equality for all four metrics on a perfect solve.
The plan's own Approach uses inequality language (`>= 0.70`, `>= 0.6666666667`) for two of the five metrics, creating an ambiguity that does not exist in the prior art.
findings.finding: The `calibration_metrics` AC specifies that 'an injected perfect solve returns the exact metric mapping specified in the Approach,' but the Approach specifies release thresholds as inequalities for kappa (`>= 0.70`) and per-case stability (`>= 0.6666666667`) rather than exact values. For 4 fire + 4 no-fire balanced cases with a perfect solve, kappa is deterministically `1.0` — but the plan never states this, and the AC's 'exact metric mapping' language conflicts with the inequality framing of the Approach. An implementer must decide: should the test assert `kappa == 1.0` (deducible from `cohens_kappa` math) or `kappa >= 0.70` (the Approach threshold)?
findings.location: Approach section — release threshold table; AC: `calibration_metrics` test
scenarios: An implementer writes `assert metrics['kappa'] >= 0.70` (matching the Approach threshold language) but the prior-art pattern and the AC's 'exact' language suggest `assert metrics['kappa'] == 1.0`.
A reviewer reading the AC cannot determine from the plan text alone whether the `calibration_metrics` test should use equality or inequality assertions for kappa and stability.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the inequality framing for kappa and stability in the Approach's metric table with: 'An injected perfect solve must return `recall == 1.0`, `false_accept == 0.0`, `agreement == 1.0`, `kappa == 1.0`, `stability_min == 1.0`; the release gate thresholds (kappa >= 0.70, stability >= 0.667) apply to the live operator-attested run, not to the injected deterministic test.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the `spec_contract` test (or a dedicated scorer-registration check) calls `validate_eval_spec(spec, strict=True)` so that the `emits_valid_findings` scorer name is verified against the registered scorer registry offline.
evidence: eval.py:275-320: validate_eval_spec without strict=True only checks scorer type and name non-emptiness — it does NOT check against known_scorer_names()
eval.py:311-316: 'if strict: from rebar.llm.evals.eval_scorers import known_scorer_names; known = known_scorer_names()' — registration check gated on strict=True
Plan says: 'The new structural tests use the real eval loader plus validate_eval_spec' — no mention of strict=True
eval_scorers.py:404: 'emits_valid_findings' IS registered, so strict mode would pass — but the gap is about future-proofing the offline gate
findings.finding: The structural test's use of validate_eval_spec is not specified as strict=True, meaning the deterministic scorer name 'emits_valid_findings' will not be checked against the registered scorer registry offline. If a future edit misspells the scorer name, the spec_contract test passes but the scorer silently no-ops at live-eval time.
findings.location: Approach section (structural tests paragraph) and AC: 'The eval spec declares one deterministic emits_valid_findings scorer. Prove with ... -k spec_contract'
scenarios: A scorer name is edited to 'emits_valid_finding' (typo) — lenient validate_eval_spec passes, spec_contract AC is green, but the scorer silently produces no ScoreResult at live eval time
A future refactor renames the scorer in the registry — the structural test doesn't catch the drift
findings.suggested_fix: In the test that proves the spec_contract AC, add: `assert ev.validate_eval_spec(spec, strict=True) == []` (the spec has a dataset and gold_set, so strict mode is applicable and will additionally verify scorer registration).

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
